1.2 Inverse of Relations and Their Graph: domain of inverse equals range of original
range of inverse equals domain of original

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The graph of a relation is given by the inequality \(y \geq x + 2\). What is the range of this relation?
questions.hint: For any \(y\), we can choose \(x = y - 2\) to satisfy \(y \geq x + 2\).
questions.explanation: The inequality \(y \geq x + 2\) can be rearranged to \(x \leq y - 2\). For any real \(y\), choose \(x = y - 2\) (or any smaller \(x\)), and the inequality holds. Thus the range is all real numbers. Option A is correct.

explanations_tiered.definition: The range of a relation is the set of all second components that actually appear among its ordered pairs.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For \(y \geq x + 2\), given any \(y\), we can select \(x = y - 2\) (which satisfies \(x \leq y-2\)) to get a valid ordered pair. Hence every real \(y\) is in the range.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Pick any number for \(y\), say 10. You can find an \(x\) (like 8) so that \(y \geq x+2\) is true. So every \(y\) works.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Rewrite as \(x \leq y-2\). For a fixed \(y\), choosing \(x = y-2\) satisfies \(y \geq (y-2) + 2 = y\). Since \(y\) is arbitrary, range = \(\mathbb{R}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The inequality \(y \geq x+2\) describes all points on or above the line \(y = x+2\). Projecting this region onto the y-axis covers all real numbers because the line has slope 1 and extends infinitely in both y-directions.
reasoning_steps: This pair satisfies \(y \geq x+2\), so \(y\) is in the range.
misconceptions: Assuming the range is only the boundary line \(y=x+2\), which would be all reals anyway but missing points above.
Thinking the range is limited to values where the line crosses the y-axis (y≥2) — that would be true for domain restrictions but not here.

glossary_entry.definition: The set of all y-values for which there exists some x satisfying the inequality.

flashcard.front: What is the range of \(y \geq x+2\)?
flashcard.back: \(\mathbb{R}\). For any \(y\), choose \(x = y-2\) to satisfy the inequality.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the range of \(y \geq x+2\)?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: \(\mathbb{R}\). Every y-value can be obtained by choosing \(x = y-2\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The set of all y-values for which there exists some x satisfying the inequality.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Graph the inequality and ask: what y-values appear? All of them.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine domain and range of relations given by inequalities
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. For any real y, choose x = y-2, then y ≥ (y-2)+2 = y holds. Thus all real y appear. Option B would be range if x were fixed; Option C is incorrect; Option D is only one point.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.2 Inverse of Relations and Their Graphs
questions.subsection: Domain and Range of Inverse Relations
questions.structural_anchor: Domain and Range of Inverse Relations
questions.question: If the domain of a relation \(\mathrm{R}\) is \(\{2,4,6\}\) and its range is \(\{1,3,5\}\), what are the domain and range of \(\mathrm{R}^{-1}\) respectively?
questions.hint: The domain of \(\mathrm{R}^{-1}\) equals the range of \(\mathrm{R}\), and the range of \(\mathrm{R}^{-1}\) equals the domain of \(\mathrm{R}\).
questions.explanation: By definition, \(\operatorname{Dom}(\mathrm{R}^{-1}) = \operatorname{Ran}(\mathrm{R}) = \{1,3,5\}\) and \(\operatorname{Ran}(\mathrm{R}^{-1}) = \operatorname{Dom}(\mathrm{R}) = \{2,4,6\}\). Option B correctly gives domain then range in that order. Option A swaps them; Options C and D are incorrect.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.2 Inverse of Relations and Their Graphs
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Domain and Range of Inverse Relations
source_trace.structural_element: Domain and Range of Inverse Relations
source_trace.content_anchor: Domain of R^{-1} = Range of R, Range of R^{-1} = Domain of R

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Swapping coordinates exchanges the roles of domain and range. Therefore the domain of the inverse is the range of the original, and the range of the inverse is the domain of the original.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The inverse flips each pair. The inputs of the inverse are the outputs of the original. The outputs of the inverse are the inputs of the original.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The inverse relation is the transpose of the set of ordered pairs. The projection operators swap: \(\pi_1(R^{-1}) = \pi_2(R)\) and \(\pi_2(R^{-1}) = \pi_1(R)\).
reasoning_steps: Recall: Domain of inverse = Range of original.
Recall: Range of inverse = Domain of original.
misconceptions: Assuming domain and range stay the same after inversion.
Confusing which one swaps to which: domain(inverse) = range(original), not the other way around.

glossary_entry.definition: The set of first components of the inverse; equals the range of the original relation.

flashcard.front: If Dom(R) = {2,4,6} and Ran(R) = {1,3,5}, what are Dom(R^{-1}) and Ran(R^{-1})?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How are the domain and range of \(R^{-1}\) related to \(R\)?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Domain of inverse = range of original; range of inverse = domain of original.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The set of first components of the inverse; equals the range of the original relation.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use arrow diagrams to show reversal.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine the domain and range of inverse relations
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.2 Inverse of Relations and Their Graphs
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Domain of inverse = range of original = {1,3,5}; range of inverse = domain of original = {2,4,6}. Option A has them swapped incorrectly.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A graph passes through the points \((0,1)\), \((1,2)\), \((2,4)\), and \((2,5)\). Why does this graph fail the vertical line test?

options.A: The graph passes through the origin.
options.B: The graph has points with the same \(x\)-coordinate but different \(y\)-coordinates.
options.C: The graph has points with the same \(y\)-coordinate but different \(x\)-coordinates.
options.D: The graph has no points at \(x = 3\) and \(x = 4\).

questions.hint: A vertical line at \(x = 2\) would intersect the graph at two points: \((2,4)\) and \((2,5)\).
questions.explanation: The vertical line test states that a graph represents a function if no vertical line intersects it more than once. Here, \(x = 2\) gives two different \(y\)-values (4 and 5), so the vertical line test fails. Option B correctly identifies this issue.

source_trace.content_anchor: no two points on same vertical line

explanations_tiered.definition: The vertical line test is a graphical method to determine if a relation is a function: if any vertical line intersects the graph more than once, the graph does not represent a function.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Points \((2,4)\) and \((2,5)\) share the same \(x\)-coordinate (2) but different \(y\)-coordinates, so a vertical line at \(x=2\) hits two points. This violates the function definition.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Draw a vertical line at \(x=2\). It passes through two points, so one input gives two outputs — not allowed for a function.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The vertical line \(x=2\) intersects the graph at \((2,4)\) and \((2,5)\). Since one \(x\)-value maps to two different \(y\)-values, the graph fails the vertical line test and is not a function.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The condition for a function is that the relation is single-valued: \(\forall x \in \text{Dom}, \exists! y \text{ s.t. } (x,y) \in R\). Two points with identical first coordinate and distinct second coordinates violate uniqueness.
reasoning_steps: Identify any x-value that appears more than once among the points.
Check if the y-values for that x are different.
If yes, vertical line test fails → not a function.
misconceptions: Confusing vertical line test with horizontal line test (which tests for one-to-one functions).
Thinking that any repetition of x-values automatically fails even if y-values are same (same y is allowed because output is still unique).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Vertical line test: if any vertical line hits graph twice → not a function.

glossary_entry.definition: A visual method to determine if a graph represents a function: if any vertical line intersects the graph more than once, the graph is not a function.

flashcard.front: What does it mean if a vertical line intersects a graph at two points?
flashcard.back: The graph does not represent a function because one input gives two different outputs.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does the set \((0,1), (1,2), (2,4), (2,5)\) fail the vertical line test?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Because x=2 corresponds to both y=4 and y=5, so a vertical line at x=2 hits two points.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A visual method to determine if a graph represents a function: if any vertical line intersects the graph more than once, the graph is not a function.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Same x, different y → fails vertical line test.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that the test works because a vertical line represents a single input value.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply the vertical line test to identify functions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The vertical line at x=2 intersects at (2,4) and (2,5), so the graph fails the test. Option A is irrelevant; Option C describes horizontal line test; Option D is not relevant.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What are the domain and range of \(f(x) = x^{-\frac{1}{2}}\)?
questions.hint: \(x^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\). The square root requires \(x > 0\), and the reciprocal keeps it positive.
questions.explanation: \(f(x) = x^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\). The square root requires \(x \geq 0\), but denominator cannot be zero, so \(x > 0\). Output is positive for all \(x > 0\), so range is \((0, \infty)\). Option B is correct; option A includes 0; option C includes negatives; option D includes 0 in range incorrectly.

explanations_tiered.definition: For \(f(x) = x^{-1/n}\) with even \(n\), the domain is \((0,\infty)\) and the range is \((0,\infty)\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: \(x^{-1/2} = 1/\sqrt{x}\). The square root requires \(x \ge 0\), but division by zero excludes \(x=0\), so \(x > 0\). The output is always positive, so range is positive reals.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You can't take square root of zero in denominator, so \(x\) must be greater than zero. The result is always positive.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Rewrite as \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\). For real numbers, \(\sqrt{x}\) requires \(x \ge 0\), but \(\sqrt{0}=0\) makes the fraction undefined. Hence \(x>0\). Since \(\sqrt{x}>0\), its reciprocal is also \(>0\), so range = \((0,\infty)\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: This function is the reciprocal of the square root function. It is defined on positive reals and maps onto positive reals, with a vertical asymptote at \(x=0\) and a horizontal asymptote at \(y=0\).
reasoning_steps: Denominator involves even root → radicand must be >0 (not just ≥0 because denominator ≠0).
Output is positive for all inputs → range = (0,∞).
misconceptions: Including 0 in the domain (division by zero error).
Assuming negative exponent allows negative inputs (even root restriction still applies).

glossary_entry.term: Power function with negative even-denominator exponent
glossary_entry.definition: A function of the form \(f(x) = x^{-1/n}\) with \(n\) even; domain and range are \((0,\infty)\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is 0 not in the domain of \(x^{-1/2}\)?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Because \(x^{-1/2} = 1/\sqrt{x}\), and \(\sqrt{0}=0\) would make the denominator zero, which is undefined.
publishing_metadata.glossary_term: Power function with negative even-denominator exponent
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A function of the form \(f(x) = x^{-1/n}\) with \(n\) even; domain and range are \((0,\infty)\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare with \(x^{1/2}\) which includes 0 in domain and range.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine domain and range of power functions with negative rational exponents
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(x^{-1/2} = 1/\sqrt{x}\). Square root requires \(x \ge 0\), but denominator cannot be zero, so \(x > 0\). Output is always positive, so range = \((0,\infty)\). Option A incorrectly includes 0; Option C includes negatives; Option D includes 0 in range.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is the domain of \(f(x) = x^{-\frac{2}{3}}\)?
questions.hint: \(x^{-2/3} = \frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{x^2}}\). Cube root is defined for all reals, but denominator cannot be zero.
questions.explanation: \(f(x) = x^{-2/3} = \frac{1}{x^{2/3}} = \frac{1}{(\sqrt[3]{x})^2}\). Cube root is defined for all \(x \in \mathbb{R}\), and squaring removes sign, but denominator \(x^{2/3}\) is zero only when \(x = 0\). Thus domain is all real numbers except 0. Option D is correct.

explanations_tiered.definition: For \(f(x) = x^{-m/n}\) with even numerator and odd denominator, the domain is all nonzero reals.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Rewrite as \(1/(\sqrt[3]{x})^2\). Cube root is defined for all \(x\), but denominator is zero only at \(x=0\). Hence all real numbers except 0.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Cube root works for negative numbers, but you can't divide by zero. So \(x\) can be any number except 0.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(x^{-2/3} = \frac{1}{(x^{1/3})^2}\). The cube root \(x^{1/3}\) is defined for all real \(x\), but the denominator is zero when \(x=0\). Thus domain = \(\mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The function is even (since exponent numerator is even), so it is symmetric about the y-axis. It has a vertical asymptote at \(x=0\) and is defined for all nonzero reals.
reasoning_steps: Identify that exponent is negative → reciprocal.
Cube root defined for all real x.
Thus domain = all real numbers except 0.
misconceptions: Assuming even root in denominator (but here denominator exponent 2/3 has cube root, which is odd root, so domain includes negatives).
Excluding negative numbers incorrectly because of even exponent in numerator (numerator even only affects range, not domain when denominator is odd).

glossary_entry.term: Power function with even numerator odd denominator negative exponent
glossary_entry.definition: A function of the form \(f(x) = x^{-m/n}\) with \(m\) even and \(n\) odd; domain is all nonzero reals, range is \((0,\infty)\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Yes, because it involves a cube root (odd root), which is defined for negative numbers. Only \(x=0\) is excluded.
publishing_metadata.glossary_term: Power function with even numerator odd denominator negative exponent
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A function of the form \(f(x) = x^{-m/n}\) with \(m\) even and \(n\) odd; domain is all nonzero reals, range is \((0,\infty)\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare \(x^{-1/2}\) vs \(x^{-2/3}\): odd root in denominator changes domain.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine domain of power functions with negative rational exponents
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. \(x^{-2/3} = 1/(\sqrt[3]{x})^2\). Cube root defined for all reals, denominator zero only at x=0, so domain = \(\mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}\). Option A includes 0; Option B excludes negatives; Option C excludes negatives and 0.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the signum function \(\operatorname{sgn}(x)\) for \(x = 0\)?
questions.hint: The signum function assigns 0 to the input 0.
questions.explanation: By definition, \(\operatorname{sgn}(x) = 0\) when \(x = 0\). Option B correctly states this. Option A is for positive \(x\); Option C for negative \(x\); Option D is false because the function is defined for all reals.

explanations_tiered.definition: The signum function returns 1 for positive numbers, 0 for zero, and -1 for negative numbers.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: At \(x = 0\), the signum function is explicitly defined as 0.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Zero is neither positive nor negative, so its sign is 0.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The piecewise definition states \(\operatorname{sgn}(0) = 0\). This is consistent with \(\operatorname{sgn}(0) = \frac{0}{|0|}\) in the limit sense, though that expression is undefined directly.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The signum function is defined to make it a total function on \(\mathbb{R}\). The value at zero is chosen as 0 for continuity from the right/left in an average sense.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of signum: three cases.
misconceptions: Thinking sgn(0) is undefined (confusion with |x|/x form).
Assigning sgn(0) = 1 or -1 incorrectly.

glossary_entry.definition: A function that returns 1 for positive inputs, 0 for zero, and -1 for negative inputs.

flashcard.back: 0, by definition of the signum function.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the value of the signum function at zero?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A function that returns 1 for positive inputs, 0 for zero, and -1 for negative inputs.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Note that \(|x|/x\) is undefined at 0, so the piecewise definition is essential.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Evaluate the signum function at given points
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. By definition, sgn(0) = 0. Option A is for x>0; Option C for x<0; Option D is false.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Given \(f(x) = 2x + 1\) and \(g(x) = x^2 - 3\), what is \((f \circ g)(x)\)?
questions.explanation: \((f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x)) = f(x^2 - 3) = 2(x^2 - 3) + 1 = 2x^2 - 6 + 1 = 2x^2 - 5\). Option A matches. Option B is \((g \circ f)(x)\); Options C and D result from incorrect distribution.

explanations_tiered.definition: The composition \((f \circ g)(x)\) means apply \(g\) to \(x\), then apply \(f\) to the result.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: First compute \(g(x) = x^2 - 3\). Then substitute into \(f\): \(f(g(x)) = 2(x^2 - 3) + 1 = 2x^2 - 5\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Work from inside out: first do \(x^2 - 3\), then double it and add 1.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Composition is associative but not commutative. Here \((f \circ g)(x)\) yields a quadratic, while \((g \circ f)(x)\) yields a different quadratic.
misconceptions: Multiplying \(f\) and \(g\) instead of composing.
Confusing \((f \circ g)\) with \((g \circ f)\).

glossary_entry.definition: The operation that takes two functions \(f\) and \(g\) and produces a function \(h(x) = f(g(x))\).

flashcard.front: How do you compute \((f \circ g)(x)\) if \(f(x)=2x+1\) and \(g(x)=x^2-3\)?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The operation that takes two functions \(f\) and \(g\) and produces a function \(h(x) = f(g(x))\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize the order: \(f \circ g\) means \(g\) first, then \(f\).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. \((f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x)) = 2(x^2-3)+1 = 2x^2-5\). Option B is \((g \circ f)(x)\); Option C incorrectly distributes; Option D misses the -6 term.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Given \(f(x) = \sqrt{x}\) with domain \([0,\infty)\) and \(g(x) = x - 5\) with domain \(\mathbb{R}\), what is the domain of \((f \circ g)(x)\)?
questions.hint: \((f \circ g)(x) = \sqrt{x - 5}\). The expression under the square root must be \(\ge 0\).
questions.explanation: \((f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x)) = f(x-5) = \sqrt{x-5}\). The square root requires \(x-5 \ge 0 \Rightarrow x \ge 5\). Thus the domain is \([5,\infty)\). Option B is correct; Option A is domain of \(f\); Option C ignores restriction; Option D is incorrect.

explanations_tiered.definition: The domain of \((f \circ g)(x)\) is the set of all \(x\) in the domain of \(g\) such that \(g(x)\) is in the domain of \(f\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Here \(g(x) = x-5\) must be in the domain of \(f\), which is \([0,\infty)\). So \(x-5 \ge 0 \Rightarrow x \ge 5\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: You can only take square roots of numbers 0 or larger. So \(x-5\) must be ≥0, meaning \(x \ge 5\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \((f \circ g)(x) = \sqrt{x-5}\). For the square root to be real, we need \(x-5 \ge 0\), i.e., \(x \ge 5\). The domain is \([5,\infty)\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The composition is defined when \(x \in \operatorname{Dom}(g)\) and \(g(x) \in \operatorname{Dom}(f)\). \(\operatorname{Dom}(g) = \mathbb{R}\), and \(g(x) \in [0,\infty)\) gives \(x-5 \ge 0\). Thus domain = \([5,\infty)\).
worked_solution: Step 2: For \(\sqrt{x-5}\) to be real, require \(x-5 \ge 0\).
reasoning_steps: Determine condition for \(g(x)\) to be in domain of \(f\).
misconceptions: Using domain of \(f\) directly instead of applying condition to \(g(x)\).
Forgetting that \(g\) must also be defined (here it is always defined).

glossary_entry.definition: The set of all \(x\) such that \(x\) is in the domain of \(g\) and \(g(x)\) is in the domain of \(f\).

flashcard.back: \([5,\infty)\) because the radicand must be nonnegative.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is the domain of \(\sqrt{x-5}\) not all real numbers?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Because the square root requires the radicand \(x-5\) to be ≥0, so \(x \ge 5\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The set of all \(x\) such that \(x\) is in the domain of \(g\) and \(g(x)\) is in the domain of \(f\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Always check both functions: inner function output must be valid input for outer function.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine the domain of composite functions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \((f \circ g)(x) = \sqrt{x-5}\). Square root requires \(x-5 \ge 0\) so \(x \ge 5\). Option A is domain of \(f\); Option C ignores restriction; Option D shifts incorrectly.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If \(f(x) = 5x - 7\), which expression represents \(f^{-1}(x)\)?
questions.hint: Swap \(x\) and \(y\) in \(y = 5x - 7\), then solve for \(y\).
questions.explanation: Start with \(y = 5x - 7\). Swap: \(x = 5y - 7\). Solve: \(x + 7 = 5y \Rightarrow y = \frac{x+7}{5}\). Option A matches. Option B subtracts 7; Option C doesn't swap; Option D subtracts from 7.

source_trace.content_anchor: find inverse by swapping x and y, solving for y

explanations_tiered.definition: The inverse function \(f^{-1}\) satisfies \(f(f^{-1}(x)) = x\) and \(f^{-1}(f(x)) = x\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: To find the inverse algebraically, swap \(x\) and \(y\) in \(y = 5x - 7\) to get \(x = 5y - 7\), then solve for \(y\): \(y = (x+7)/5\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Swap \(x\) and \(y\), then get \(y\) alone. Add 7, divide by 5.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The inverse of a linear function \(f(x) = mx + b\) is \(f^{-1}(x) = \frac{x - b}{m}\), provided \(m \neq 0\). Here \(m=5, b=-7\), so \(f^{-1}(x) = \frac{x - (-7)}{5} = \frac{x+7}{5}\).
reasoning_steps: Solve the resulting equation for y.
misconceptions: Forgetting to swap before solving.
Solving for x instead of y after swapping.

glossary_entry.definition: A function \(f^{-1}\) such that \(f(f^{-1}(x)) = x\) and \(f^{-1}(f(x)) = x\) for all \(x\) in the respective domains.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A function \(f^{-1}\) such that \(f(f^{-1}(x)) = x\) and \(f^{-1}(f(x)) = x\) for all \(x\) in the respective domains.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Find the inverse of a linear function
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Swap: \(x = 5y - 7\) → \(x+7 = 5y\) → \(y = (x+7)/5\). Option B subtracts 7; Option C does not swap; Option D gives \((7-x)/5\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A function \(f\) is defined by \(f(x) = x^3 + 2\). Why is \(f\) invertible (has an inverse function)?

options.C: Its graph is symmetric about the y-axis.
options.D: Its range is all real numbers.

questions.hint: A function has an inverse if and only if it is one-to-one (passes the horizontal line test).
questions.explanation: A function is invertible if it is one-to-one (injective), meaning each output comes from exactly one input. \(f(x) = x^3 + 2\) is strictly increasing (since derivative \(3x^2 \ge 0\) and zero only at a point), so it passes the horizontal line test. Option B correctly states the condition.

source_trace.content_anchor: a function is invertible if its inverse is also a function

explanations_tiered.definition: A function is invertible if it is one-to-one (injective), meaning distinct inputs produce distinct outputs.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: \(f(x) = x^3 + 2\) is strictly increasing because the derivative \(3x^2 \ge 0\) and is zero only at a single point, so it passes the horizontal line test. Thus it is one-to-one and invertible.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The graph of \(x^3+2\) always goes up; it never repeats a y-value. That means each output has exactly one input, so we can reverse the process.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A function has an inverse if it is one-to-one. \(f(x) = x^3+2\) is strictly increasing, so if \(a \neq b\), then \(a^3+2 \neq b^3+2\). Hence it passes the horizontal line test and is invertible.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The function is bijective from \(\mathbb{R}\) to \(\mathbb{R}\). Its inverse is \(f^{-1}(x) = \sqrt[3]{x-2}\). The condition for invertibility is injectivity (one-to-one).
reasoning_steps: Recall: A function is invertible iff it is one-to-one (injective).
For \(x^3+2\), if \(a^3+2 = b^3+2 \Rightarrow a^3 = b^3 \Rightarrow a=b\).
misconceptions: Thinking all functions are invertible (only one-to-one functions are).
Confusing invertibility with having an inverse relation (every relation has an inverse, but not every function has an inverse function).

glossary_entry.definition: A function that has an inverse function; equivalently, a one-to-one (injective) function.

flashcard.front: What condition makes a function invertible?
flashcard.back: It must be one-to-one (injective), passing the horizontal line test.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It is one-to-one (strictly increasing), so it passes the horizontal line test and has an inverse.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A function that has an inverse function; equivalently, a one-to-one (injective) function.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine whether a function is invertible
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. A function is invertible if it is one-to-one. \(f(x)=x^3+2\) is strictly increasing, hence one-to-one. Option A is false (cubic, not quadratic); Option C describes even functions; Option D is insufficient (many non-invertible functions have range \(\mathbb{R}\)).
